title: 🎙️ Working Lands & Industry
description: Today's focus is the resource economy that defines the Cariboo — forestry, ranching, mining, and the agricultural technology helping these industries adapt and modernize. We're looking for stories about the people and innovations keeping rural BC's working landscape viable, including tensions between resource extraction and long-term stewardship, and the technology bridging both. - Daily themed article selection for podcast generation

_podcast.theme_description: Today's focus is the resource economy that defines the Cariboo — forestry, ranching, mining, and the agricultural technology helping these industries adapt and modernize. We're looking for stories about the people and innovations keeping rural BC's working landscape viable, including tensions between resource extraction and long-term stewardship, and the technology bridging both.
_podcast.theme_scoring_prompt: Rate how well this article fits the theme 'Working Lands & Industry' through a RURAL TECH AND INNOVATION lens. Strong fits include: precision forestry and remote sensing in Cariboo logging, AgTech and ranch management systems, mining automation and safety technology, farm sensors and soil monitoring, sawmill modernization and automation, drone surveys for resource management, IoT in agriculture and ranching, climate adaptation tech for working lands, workforce training for industrial tech, commodity tracking and supply chain tech, and how Cariboo resource industries adopt innovation. Prefer articles that bridge resource economy AND technology in rural/industrial contexts. Penalise heavily (score 0–20): international geopolitical and war news with no resource-economy connection; consumer tech and gadget reviews; wildlife encounters and nature entertainment without agricultural or working-lands relevance (a Yellowstone bear video is not a working-lands story); scientific discoveries without direct application to forestry, agriculture, or extractive industry; crime and judicial news; financial market stories. Score 0-100 where 100 is a perfect thematic match and 0 is completely unrelated.

items.title: [Atlas Obscura] ‘The Devil is Loose’ in Middelfart, Denmark
items.content_html:
In 2012, the devil was officially let loose in Middelfart, Denmark. Local sculptor Steffan Herrik unveiled his bronze Fanden er løs ('The Devil is Loose') at a seafront spot that the artist nonetheless calls "the town's ugliest." Herrik describes the work as a monument to the early 2000s and their spirit of chaos and uncertainty.

Less a conventional devil than an unsettling chimera, the gaunt figure stands just under 5 feet tall (1.5 meters) and has a beaked, cyclopean head, branching antlers, and a single wing. Beneath it sits a large egg from which two snakes emerge, one with a smaller snake slipping from its open mouth like a tongue.

The title echoes the distinctly Danish saying Fanden er løs i Laksegade ("The devil is loose in Salmon Street"), rooted in an 1826 Copenhagen incident widely blamed on the devil himself. Whether or not Herrik had that case in mind, the connection feels natural in a Danish context.

For those amused by the town's name: Middelfart derives from the old Danish middel ("middle") and fart ("crossing" or "passage"), referring to the narrowest point of the Little Belt strait. As for the sulfurous associations, they are purely coincidental, though perhaps not entirely unfitting, given sulfur's long reputation as the smell of the demonic.
items.summary: In 2012, the devil was officially let loose in Middelfart, Denmark. Local sculptor Steffan Herrik unveiled his bronze Fanden er løs ('The Devil is Loose') at a seafront spot that the artist nonetheless calls "the town's ugliest." Herrik describes the work as a monument to the early 2000s and their
